content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886
June 25, 2002

Anne A. Lee
Foley Lardner
402 W. Broadway, Suite 2300
San Diego, CA 92101-3542

Re:     Your Request for Informal Assistance
        Our File No.  I-02-160

Dear Ms. Lee:

     This letter is in response to your request for follow-up advice on behalf of
Monterey Park City Councilmember Benjamin Frank Venti regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     The facts are substantially the same as those in the prior request from your office.
Councilmember Venti would like to request reconsideration of a towing contract.
Mr. Venti owns a home that is located approximately 400 feet away from the towing
company's offices, but because of the topography (his house is on a hill), the two are
more than a mile apart by car.

     Mr. Venti also owns a 4% interest in and is a member of the loan committee of
Golden Security Bank ("Bank"). The Bank has approximately $100 million in assets and
loan originations of over $24 million per year. Several years ago, the Bank lent $150,000
to a business located on property next door to the towing company's offices, secured by a
second trust deed. The business located there does not compete with the towing company
in any way.

     You request follow-up advice only with respect to the council member's real
property. You enclosed with your request a revised appraisal letter from Benchmark
Appraisal, which concludes that the decision in question will not have any financial effect
on the council member's property, in part due to the fact that the use of the subject
property will not change as a result of the decision and the difference in elevation of the
two properties. The council member's property is several hundred feet above the subject
property and is separated by one mile of roadway.

     As we noted in our prior letter the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, we cannot evaluate the

¹ Government Code §§ 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, §§ 18109-
18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. 1-02-160
Page No. 2

sufficiency of a modified appraisal, and cannot therefore add to our previous advice. As
we stated previously:

"A public official may seek third-party assistance, such as a
real estate appraisal, to help decide whether the presumption holds
true in his or her situation. We have advised in the context of
indirectly involved real property that an appraisal conducted by a
disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate professional,
which is based upon an accurate understanding of the underlying
facts, and also considers the factors listed in regulation
18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C), will generally be considered as a good faith
effort by a public official to assess the financial effect of a decision
on his or her property. (Perkins Advice Letter, No. A-99-024.)

"However, a public official may not simply rely on a third-
party appraisal without further inquiry into whether the person
conducting the appraisal is qualified to do so, whether the appraisal
considered the appropriate standards and all of the appropriate
factors described in our regulations, and whether the conclusion
reached by the appraiser is objectively defensible (e.g., based on a
full and accurate assessment of the underlying facts). (O'Harra
Advice Letter, A-00-174.) When these criteria are met, a public
official may find that a third-party appraisal provides a reasonable
and objective basis for rebutting the presumption that a decision
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon
his or her economic interest in directly-involved real property."
(Moser Advice Letter, 1-02-114.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

Enclosure
JWW:jg

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Monterey Park City Councilmember may participate in a decision regarding a towing company contract when he owns real property approximately 400 feet away (by topography over a mile by car) and has a 40% interest in a bank that lent money to a nearby business.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot evaluate the sufficiency of a revised appraisal or add to its previous advice, but reaffirms that a public official may rely on a qualified third-party appraisal if it considers appropriate regulatory factors and the conclusion is objectively defensible based on accurate facts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains substantive legal analysis and guidance. The document is a follow-up to a prior advice letter (I-02-114/Moser) and primarily restates previously provided guidance rather than offering new conclusions. No explicit section headers present in parsing.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a Monterey Park City Councilmember may participate in a decision regarding a towing company contract when he owns real property approximately 400 feet away (by topography over a mile by car) and has a 40% interest in a bank that lent money to a nearby business.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC cannot evaluate the sufficiency of a revised appraisal or add to its previous advice, but reaffirms that a public official may rely on a qualified third-party appraisal if it considers appropriate regulatory factors and the conclusion is objectively defensible based on accurate facts.

embedding.summary: FPPC follow-up advice letter to counsel for Monterey Park Councilmember Benjamin Frank Venti regarding conflict-of-interest provisions and a towing contract decision involving the councilmember's nearby real property and banking interests. The FPPC declines to evaluate the sufficiency of a revised appraisal but restates prior guidance on using third-party appraisals to rebut presumptions of financial effect.
